message.reasoning_content:
We need to extract HVS candidates from paper 1901.04559. The paper is about Sagittarius stream star identification using APOGEE abundances. The method is chemical tagging, but they also find one hypervelocity star (HVS) candidate. The catalog_review summary mentions "hypervelocity star candidate". The ECSV extraction is empty (skipped), so no ECSV tables. We need to use the paper text directly. The paper mentions in the abstract: "We also find one hypervelocity star candidate." In Section 4.4 (HVS) they describe it. The star is part of the "Sgr chemical sample", so it's one of the 68 chemical candidates. They say: "One star in the Sgr chemical sample... is at a Galactocentric distance of ~54 kpc... total Galactocentric space velocity of ~577^{+209}_{-129} km/s... orbit integration suggests ... completely unbound from the Milky Way." They also mention its Gaia proper motions, distance, radial velocity, etc. The star's APOGEE ID is likely in Table 1, but the LaTeX table only shows a few entries and then "... ** The full list is available from the online journal." So we don't have the APOGEE ID from the paper text. However, the paper might mention which one is the HVS? The text does not give the APOGEE ID explicitly. But we need to identify at least one candidate. Since we don't have the full table, we need to extract the HVS candidate based on the text description. The extraction skill says: "candidates list must be exhaustive: include every object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way ... never truncate, sample, or pick representatives". But we have only one candidate, which is the HVS. However, the paper also might be ambiguous about other objects; they say that group of "accreted halo" etc., but those are not claimed unbound, only high eccentric orbits but bound. So only the HVS is unbound. So we have one candidate.

We need to fill the method_chain for the paper-level method that leads to the HVS candidate and the chemical tagging leading to its selection. The method chain: inputs: APOGEE DR14, Sgr core sample from Hasselquist2017, Gaia DR2 proper motions. Steps: sample selection (stellar parameter cuts, S/N, etc.), chemical clustering (k-means on 6 abundance ratios) to select chemical candidates (68 stars), then orbit integration using galpy with MWPotential2014 and LM10 potential, and then classification into groups based on orbital properties: Sgr stream, accreted halo, bulge, HVS, others. For the HVS, they also assess unbound via orbit integration, discuss origin.

We need to extract as much data as possible for the HVS. The paper gives: Galactocentric distance ~54 kpc, Galactocentric space velocity 577 +209/-129 km/s, total velocity probably v_tot in Galactocentric rest frame. They mention radial velocity v_gsr = -111 km/s (line 402). Gaia proper motions: (mu_alpha cos delta, mu_delta) = (-1.91, -0.10) ± (0.62, 0.44) mas/yr. Distance error ~15%, but no exact distance value. They mention that orbit integration unbound. They mention "closest approach will occur in ~20 Myr, at r_peri ~53 kpc". So peri- and apo- center. Also they mention it is unbound in MWPotential2014.

The paper does not give a table for this star, but we can treat the text as the source. The candidate origin: this star's HVS status is first proposed in this paper? The abstract says "We also find one hypervelocity star candidate", so introduced_by_this_paper. But we need to see if they cite any prior work; they discuss other HVS theories but not that this object was previously known as HVS. So introduced.

Identifiers: We have no APOGEE ID from the paper text, but the star is from the Sgr chemical cluster of 68 candidates. So we can set paper_candidate_id as a placeholder based on description, maybe "HVS in Sgr chemical sample"? The skill says: Prefer stable identifiers in this order: Gaia source ID, explicit object name, paper candidate number, and only then a table-row relation documented by the paper. The paper does not give a name, no Gaia source ID in text. There is a table, but we don't have full table, so we can't get the APOGEE ID. We can perhaps use the catalog review's mention that there is a hypervelocity star candidate, but that is metadata. We need to extract from the paper. Since we don't have the full table, we might need to note that the candidate ID is not available in the extracted text. However, the extraction skill requires us to fill identifiers: record_id (we can assign), paper_candidate_id (we need something), gaia_source_id (unknown). All[] with source refs. Since the paper does not provide a specific identifier in the excerpt we have, we could label it as "HVS candidate in Sgr chemical cluster" with context refs to the text. But that's not a paper-visible name. Alternatively, we can look at the LaTeX table; the table shows many APOGEE IDs like "2M00151324-1430281" etc. The HVS is likely one of those, but we don't know which. The text says "The catalog of APOGEE IDs and membership assignments is shown in Table 1". But we only see the beginning of table. The group d = HVS. So the table entry for membership would be "d". The example entries show membership letters a,b,c,d,e. So one row would have membership "d". We don't know which APOGEE ID corresponds to that. So we cannot extract the specific APOGEE ID. But the paper states that the full list is available online. The extraction might need to be partial. The validation may require paper_candidate_id. We should set paper_candidate_id as something like "HVS candidate (unknown APOGEE ID)", but that's not a paper-visible identifier. Maybe we can note that the candidate is identified only by its property as the unbound star. However, the skill says: "Use paper text and LaTeX source for candidate rationale... values missing from ECSV." The text refers to "this star", never identifies it. We can create an identifier like "2M...? (unknown, see Table 1 HVS)". But we must provide a paper-visible name if possible. Actually, we can check if the paper mentions any reference to previous studies? No. So we have no identifier. However, the extraction skill acknowledges that the identifiers can be incomplete; it requires record_id and paper_candidate_id. So we need to create a paper_candidate_id that reflects the paper's reference to it. Perhaps we can use a generic label like "HVS-1", but that's not paper-originated. Alternatively, we can assign "Table1-d" meaning the row with membership d in Table 1. The schema expects a string for paper_candidate_id, and we can describe that the exact APOGEE ID is not given in the excerpt. I'll set paper_candidate_id to "Table 1 membership 'd' HVS" and note in inclusion_assessment that the exact APOGEE ID is in the full table. But we need to cite the paper source refs for membership designation. So we can use the text that mentions the HVS (1 star) and the table caption with membership d = HVS. So paper_candidate_id could be "2M???... (HVS from Table 1, membership d)". But I'll try to find if the table lists any star with membership d? The table only shows a few rows, all with membership a. So not. So we cannot extract the actual ID. So I'll set paper_candidate_id to a placeholder indicating the membership d from Table 1, with source ref to the table and text. That is still paper-visible. We'll note in the summary that the exact APOGEE ID is not available due to the full table not being in the arXiv source. The extraction status can be "partial" if we can't get all, but we can still include the candidate with the available data.

Now, we need to create method_chain. From the paper: 
- step-01: input catalog APOGEE DR14, Gaia DR2 proper motions, Sgr core sample from Hasselquist2017.
- step-02: sample selection from APOGEE DR14: Teff 3550-4200 K, S/N>70, no ASPCAPBAD flag, [Fe/H] > -1.2, distances from DR14 VAC.
- step-03: cross-match with Gaia DR2 to get proper motions for 62/68 candidates.
- step-04: quality filter applied (no additional). 
- step-05: distance estimation: use DR14 VAC distances (derived by Hayden2014 methods, accurate to ~20%).
- step-06: radial velocity measurement: APOGEE spectra radial velocities (ASPACAP pipeline).
- step-07: solar position and motion: R0=8.0 kpc, circular velocity 220 km/s, solar motion from Schönrich2010.
- step-08: velocity calculation: derive Galactocentric positions and velocities using proper motions, distances, and RVs, with solar motion.
- step-09: galactic potential model: MWPotential2014 (from galpy) and Law & Majewski 2010 potential (via gala). They state MWPotential2014 parameters: bulge, disk, NFW halo.
- step-10: orbit integration: using galpy or gala, backwards and forwards 1.5 Gyr, compute orbital parameters (ecc, r_peri, r_apo, etc.)
- step-11: escape or bound assessment: for HVS, it was found unbound; no probabilities but qualitative assessment via orbit integration.
- step-12: origin assessment: discussion of possible origins, but not quantified.

We'll build the chain accordingly.

Now, fill candidate details. We'll use the provided values.

Observed phase space:
- ra, dec: not given. We don't have coordinates. So we can leave null or absent. Since required? The schema shows ra and dec are optional? In CandidateCore, observed_phase_space is required, but RA, Dec, etc. are optional. So we can omit them.
- distance: they mention Galactocentric distance ~54 kpc, but not heliocentric distance. However, they might have distance from the VAC. We'll check text: "One star in the Sgr chemical sample is at a Galactocentric distance of ~54 kpc". So we can record distance as Galactocentric radius, not heliocentric. The observed distance is heliocentric, but not provided. We can add an extra field if needed. But we can record Galactocentric radius as derived. So we'll put nothing for observed distance.
- proper motion ra and dec: from Gaia: (-1.91, -0.10) ± (0.62, 0.44) mas/yr. So we can record these under observed. The units mas/yr.
- radial velocity: v_gsr = -111 km/s. That's Galactocentric standard of rest? Actually, they say "its v_gsr = -111 km s^{-1}" in line 402. That's a measure of velocity relative to the local standard of rest? Actually, they define v_gsr earlier as Galactocentric radial velocity? In the kinematic selection section, they use v_gsr as the radial velocity in Galactic standard of rest. So it's a derived kinematic quantity. The actual observed radial velocity is presumably from APOGEE, but not given. So we can record radial_velocity as derived, but maybe it's the APOGEE radial velocity transformed to GSR. However, the paper seems to quote v_gsr directly as the observed quantity? In the abstract they mention velocities. In Sec. 4.4 they say "though its v_gsr = -111 km s^{-1} alone is much larger...". So that might be the observed radial velocity corrected to GSR. So we can treat it as derived_kinematics. The observed radial velocity from APOGEE is not given. So we can skip observed radial velocity, or note not reported. I'll omit observed radial velocity.

Derived kinematics: 
- galactocentric radius: ~54 kpc
- galactocentric velocity total: 577^{+209}_{-129} km/s. That's total velocity in Galactocentric rest frame. We can call it total_velocity or galactic_rest_frame_velocity. They say "total Galactocentric space velocity", so galactic_rest_frame_velocity. We'll record as total_velocity with value 577, upper_error 209, lower_error 129. They also give radial velocity v_gsr = -111 km/s which is a component. But we don't have other components.
- peri-center: r_peri ~53 kpc (they say closest approach r_peri ~53 kpc). So we can put under orbit. 
- time to pericenter: ~20 Myr.
- apocenter: They say it is near the most recent apocenter of Sgr orbit, and orbit integration suggests it is at apocenter? Actually, they say "this star is at a Galactocentric distance of ~54 kpc, near the position of the most recent apocenter of the LM10 Sgr orbit... Contrary to expectations for tidal debris at apocenter, this star has a total Galactocentric space velocity of ...". So its current distance might be its apocenter. But they also compute orbit that yields r_peri=53 kpc, and they say it's unbound, so not really bound. So orbit eccentricity would be >1. But they didn't report orbital parameters. So we'll put orbit fields as null. We'll note peri and time to peri as pericenter and flight_time? They said closest approach will occur in ~20 Myr, at r_peri ~53 kpc. That suggests the orbit is hyperbolic and that's its distance of closest approach (pericenter) even though it's unbound. So we can record orbital pericenter as ~53 kpc. 

Bound assessment:
- escape_velocity: not given. 
- unbound_probability: not quantitative, but they say "completely unbound". So we can set bound_probability to 0, unbound_probability to 1? But schema expects a number 0-1, unitless. But they don't give a formal probability. We can set bound_probability as 0.0 with raw_value "0" and source ref, and unbound_probability 1.0, with note that qualitative assessment. That's okay if we have a clear statement. The paper says "it is completely unbound from the Milky Way" (line 390). So bound_probability=0.0, unbound_probability=1.0, with raw_value "completely unbound". But raw_value should be the exact text. So we can record raw_value "completely unbound from the Milky Way" for unbound_probability, and note that we assigned numeric 1.0. However, the schema for bound_probability/unbound_probability expects numeric value. So we can set value: 1.0, raw_value: "completely unbound from the Milky Way". Same for bound_probability set to 0.0 with raw_value "completely unbound". This is a bit of an inference, but acceptable.

Include photometry: not given. stellar parameters: they mention "early M-giant", so spectral type maybe. They don't give Teff, etc. So we can add spectral type to stellar_parameters.spectral_type with raw_value "early M-giant", value "M", description. Also add to spectroscopy maybe.

Abundances: They say it has abundances consistent with Sgr (i.e., Sgr-like). So we can note that, but not specific values.

Quality flags: none.

Orbit: pericenter 53 kpc, time to pericenter 20 Myr. Also they note it is unbound, so eccentricity >1, but not given.

Astrophysical origin: they discuss possible origins, but conclude unlikely from black hole, etc., suggest dynamical interaction. They say "most likely origin ... dynamical interaction". So we can record origin_site as "unknown", origin_classification as "dynamical ejection?" with methods. They mention the object is in Sgr debris location, but not necessarily Sgr origin. So we can note.

Extra: maybe we can add the full total velocity error ranges, etc.

Candidate origin: introduced_by_this_paper. So we need to set origin_type as introduced_by_this_paper, and paper_reassesses_unbound_status false? Since no prior work, we can set false. We'll provide source_refs to the HVS subsection.

Inclusion assessment: summary of why this star is included, paper_labels: ["hvs_candidate"] (they call it hypervelocity star candidate). They also might label it as "unbound_star"? The text says "it is completely unbound from the Milky Way." So we can add "unbound_star" as well. galactic_bound_claim: "unbound". inclusion_basis: "explicit_unbound_text" and maybe "candidate_table_with_text_anchor" because they refer to table 1 membership d. extraction_confidence: high because clearly described.

Now, candidate groups considered: since we have one candidate from the Sgr chemical cluster, we need to note that we reviewed the Sgr stream candidates group (Table 1) and only one is HVS. So we should have a candidate_group_considered for the Sgr stream cluster, decision: "excluded" for most, but we might note that only one star was considered as unbound. But since we extracted one candidate, we don't need to list groups for excluded ones? The skill says candidate_groups_considered records tables or object groups that were reviewed and why they were excluded. We include for the rest of the cluster that we reviewed but are not HVS. So we can list the Sgr cluster as a group, and note that it contains 62 stars, with most being bound (stream and halo) and only one HVS. But we've already extracted that one. So we might need candidate_groups_considered for groups that were considered but not all are HVS candidates? The extraction schema says it's required, and if there are no candidates, we list them. For this paper with one candidate, we could still list the group as a whole and say that it was reviewed and only one out is HVS. But the schema might expect groups that were considered for HVS/unbound status. The paper doesn't explicitly group stars for unbound status; they just find one HVS among the chemical cluster. So we might not have candidate_groups_considered for HVS aside from the single star. However, the schema requires candidate_groups_considered array, even if empty? The top-level shape includes candidate_groups_considered as a required field, but not required to be non-empty. In the no_candidates case, it must be non-empty. But for candidates_found, it can be empty. We'll include an entry for the Sgr chemical sample group, describing that it contained 68 stars, but only one is unbound HVS, so the rest are excluded from HVS list. That's fine.

Now, we need to cite source refs properly. We'll read the TeX file lines: The abstract line 134 mentions "one hypervelocity star candidate". The HVS section lines 388-403 provide details. For the table, line 305-324 shows the table start, with caption and some rows. The key "d = HVS" in table caption notes line 321. So we can cite line 321 for membership assignment.

We'll need to locate line numbers exactly. Let's check the line numbers of the HVS subsection. The section starts at line 388: "\\subsection{HVS}". Then text follows. We'll cite from 388 to 403 (end of HVS section). The total velocity and proper motion details are in line 390-402.

Now, build the JSON output.
